data.title: Meta: The Market's Surprising Pessimism Offers Us A Solid Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Meta: Feeling The Pressure To Justify AI Spending Without A Cloud Business

Well, if you are a Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) investor, I guess I could safely assume that you are feeling somewhat under the weather recently. Following the post-earnings surge that META mustered, the market appears to have reversed those gains, as the stock is now grappling with its pre-earnings level.

I guess the market is now trying to assess what these new imperatives in a higher capital expenditure outlay environment really mean for META investors. Should we expect this uplift to continue into the foreseeable future, or could this just be a one-time elevated metric? And I think the situation is further complicated by the fact that Meta needs to justify the right to spend an increasingly exorbitant amount in AI CapEx without the comfort of having a fast-growing cloud computing business like that of its hyperscaler peers.

I indicated in my previous write-up on META that it surprisingly underperformed. I noted that while the company is spending heavily on AI, it has the monetization figures to validate them. Yet, the market doesn’t seem to be convinced because of the increasingly more aggressive spending that the company continues to present while not having given us much clarity on its model releases or AI revenue opportunities beyond that of its core ad business.

Which is why we see that Meta has been trying to expand its tentacles into the hardware arena, assuming the potential benefits and advantages that come with owning a wide-ranging platform. Because while we can safely say that Meta is undisputably the largest social media company by revenue in the US, it is still not widely considered a platform company when juxtaposed against that of Apple (AAPL), which owns the iOS walled garden, or Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), which operates the Android ecosystem.

Meta's Transition To A Platform Business Faces Apple's Dominant Grasp

In Meta’s case, it does seem as if it could be simply construed as an application within these ecosystems, no? I think this narrative is aligned with what CEO Mark Zuckerberg is fervently pursuing right now. Meta arguably recognized that owning the hardware distribution ecosystem may not just be fundamental but could possibly be existential in defining the success of its future business model.

And this has been the main frustration for Zuckerberg over the years, as he tries to extricate the company from Apple’s dominant platform grasp. Nevertheless, while Meta has indeed been able to capitalize on using AI effectively in its core advertising business, as we saw in the recent fourth-quarter earnings scorecard, the shift towards a platform business appears to be very much still a work in progress that Meta hasn’t fully convinced the market of yet. This narrative was recently expounded by Stratechery’s Ben Thompson in a podcast:

It’s a huge concern, and this is one of the great ironies of Meta/Facebook is the extent to which, of course, Mark and everyone hates Apple for lots of, I think, very justifiable reasons, but Apple saved Facebook from itself. Back in the day, remember Facebook Platform and there’s like Facebook Payments and all this sort of thing and Mark has always wanted to build a platform, and if you’re just an app on a phone, you can’t build a platform. The problem is that I think being an advertising-based model is generally incompatible with being a platform, the whole point of a platform is you’re letting something else shine, something else to bring to the surface, the support structure for something to take over. So an operating system is not about the — ideally, it’s the application on top of it that you’re using. When Facebook was forced to not be a platform, but just be an app, suddenly they could be fully leaning into being an advertising thing. Think about a Facebook ad, even back in the day when it was a feed ad or a story ad, literally your entire device is all an ad, and somehow it’s not a banner, that’s a little thing on the edge. They literally have achieved permission from users to take over your entire device to show you a full screen ad every five seconds, it’s amazing, and they were forced into it by Apple. - An Interview with Ben Thompson by John Collison on the Cheeky Pint Podcast

Which is why I think we are seeing tremendous progress being made in the smart glasses arena, and also seeing the company now making a concerted effort to mount a more decisive response in the smart watches segment. These are expected to be scaling faster from 2026, which could provide the company an opportune opening into what the future of AI devices could really look like, beyond just the current smartphone iteration that we are seeing right now, which is owned by the duopoly of Apple’s iOS and Google’s Android.

And Apple is not standing still. Cupertino is reportedly ramping up its efforts to infuse AI into its devices. Considering that Apple already owns more than 2.5 billion active devices globally, Meta might not be a match for Apple if AAPL can proliferate and imbibe AI quickly, hampering Meta’s efforts to scale its production capacity and outreach. Therefore, I think the market is seemingly giving Meta’s renewed goals in its hardware segment a mixed report card, since the Reality Labs business is still burning cash massively, amounting to >$19 billion in operating losses in the past twelve months.

And that seems to be the Achilles heel that is seemingly stymying Meta’s efforts to upend the clout that Apple has on the social media company in its current form. And then when we consider the lack of a fast-growing cloud business that Alphabet has built up, perhaps it is no surprise that the company is now putting most of its chips into Meta Superintelligence, the highly secretive and elite unit that’s responsible for driving and directing Meta’s whole-of-company efforts in reaching personal superintelligence beyond what we can really know of.

I guess this is precisely the case why Meta Platforms wants to push the envelope on its CapEx outlay. Because I believe Mark Zuckerberg is convinced that unless META scales quickly with compute to be at the most cutting edge of AI capabilities, the long-term trajectory of the company in advertising might not be enough to fulfill his platform dreams.

It wasn’t too long ago that the metaverse was the North Star that bound the company’s ambitions and outlook, before AI took over the world, and then that became front and center, including for Zuckerberg. Yes, I think it is sufficient to say that Anthropic (ANTHRO) and OpenAI (OPENAI), and even Google, have been making waves on the AI front, but much less Meta, even if the company is priming itself for a series of AI model releases this year.

Meta's CapEx Spending Could Hamper Margins For A While

With that in mind, it does suggest that Meta’s free cash flow margins could be structurally below the averages that it attained pre-2025, before it embarked on the current AI spending, which one might align with profligacy if the intended ROIs don’t quite pan out in the next few years.

Based on the updated Wall Street estimates, Meta could mount a revival in FCF margins from 2027 back to the mid-teen zone. However, I believe that it is still highly uncertain because it depends on how aggressive the hyperscalers intend to maintain their capital expenditure cadence. And also, it depends very much on how quickly Meta could continue to lift its core ad metrics while facing a shift in AI shopping habits to AI agents. How it changes the behavior of Meta’s e-commerce advertisers and customers as they respond to the foundational shift in behavior is now becoming more uncertain.

In contrast, the market has tuned up their confidence in Google’s business model, as we saw with the premium on GOOGL’s stock right now (being valued at 26.3x as compared to META’s 21.3x). In the near term, I believe this is probably going to persist until Meta delivers an even stronger improvement in ROI on its ad business, or expands its revenue streams into subscriptions to justify increased AI CapEx spending. Falling short of it, the underperformance in META could very well still continue for a while.

Buy Or Sell META Stock?

Despite the post-earnings reversal, I think it is important to highlight that META stock hasn’t crashed below its November lows. Therefore, I think it suggests that the market is still holding on to the bullish narrative on META, giving time for Zuckerberg and his team to showcase what they have in store for this year before they deliver judgment on whether to de-rate the stock further.

There is no doubt that META has underperformed the market over the past one year and also significantly below GOOGL’s breathtaking run. Yet, for all this skepticism, it’s critical for us to note that the stock has resiliently maintained the uptrend bias. In other words, I view this as an opportune moment for us to get even more aggressively engaged with META while the rest look away.

With META’s forward multiple of 26x broadly aligned to its 5Y average of 22x, it is time for us to savor this moment before it slips from our hands again.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Ennis: Upgrading To Buy On Growing Margins And Free Cash Flow
data.description:
Intro

Our most recent commentary on Ennis, Inc. (EBF) was in November of last year, when we pointed to margin gains as a sign of renewed stability in the printing-related US outfit. This company has a rich history, starting as a single newspaper press over a century ago and becoming the largest manufacturer of wholesale printing in the United States. Therefore, whether one is talking about printing needs in the likes of packaging, tags, labels, envelopes, forms, or digital, etc., Ennis's wide array of brands (Atlas, CFC, FMI, NorthStar, etc.) has got the market covered both from a geographical and printing-need standpoint. Although the digital revolution has been encroaching into the printing industry in recent times, the likes of advertising, photos, security documents, folders, etc., still carry weight where tangible products remain in demand.

Although we reiterated the 'Hold' rating at the time, we did not dismiss the possibility of further gains given a sound dividend and robust free cash-flow trends going forward. Fast forward approximately 12 weeks, and shares have managed to gain roughly 20%, which is an excellent return considering the almost 5% dividend yield shareholders continue to earn (Last Dividend payment was on 2/5/2026 at $0.25 per share). Remember, as reiterated in previous commentary, EBF remains an excellent dividend-paying stock, sporting a 36-year record of consecutive dividend payments and occasional 'special' payouts (the most recent special dividend was on 10/11/2024 for $2.50 a share)

5-Year Technical Chart

As we see from EBF's intermediate technical chart below, excellent share-price action over the latter part of 2025, coupled with strong year-to-date gains this year, has resulted in a bullish moving-average crossover of EBF's 10-week above its 40-week counterpart. Furthermore, the fact that consolidation in 2025 enabled the intermediate ADX trend-following indicator to subside gives a further argument that further gains are a distinct possibility at this juncture. Therefore, let's go through some fundamental reasoning on why we are upgrading our rating in EBF to a 'Buy'

Q3 Earnings And New Acquisition

EBF's third-quarter earnings release (12/22/2025) was taken well by investors, where GAAP earnings of $0.42 per share were reported on revenues of $100.2 million. Furthermore, the gross profit print of 31.9% (Key metric grew both sequentially and compared to Q3 2024) was an excellent result considering the number of acquisitions EBF has brought onto its books in recent times. The latest purchase has been CFC Print & Mail, where Ennis once more will aim to produce synergies at speed, resulting in that key gross margin metric continuing to rise over time.

We are excited to welcome CFC’s employees, customers and distributor partners into the Ennis family. The alignment of our strategic priorities, including quality, responsiveness and national scale, makes this an excellent fit.

So what makes CFC Print & Mail an excellent fit for Ennis going forward? Firstly, Ennis is already doing everything CFC is doing (Automotive, Envelopes, Education, etc.), so customers (being the 15th largest trade printer in the US) will immediately come on to books, producing the above-mentioned synergies at speed. The fact that CFC made its name through its swift national service from its Texas-based facility is also a plus for current customers, in that (given Ennis' multiple locations), we expect service to be improved on, all things remaining equal.

Essentially, though, Ennis, through its management team, will have sized up in detail the costs that will be extracted from CFC's business to ensure margins remain elevated. The endgame is essentially to bring these new customers onto the books, simultaneously paying off the undisclosed acquisition purchase price in record time.

Suffice it to say, Ennis continues to buck the trend here with respect to having the wherewithal to grow its margins, albeit in a high-spending environment. To this point, organic growth is always the preferred growth path of companies due to higher returns over time, all things remaining equal. However, even over the past nine months, Ennis has managed to grow its gross profit by roughly $2.4 million over the corresponding period in 2024. These trends are a testament to the operational efficiencies of Ennis, as well as how management has been selective in acquiring companies that add to overall margins, as the CEO laid out in the Q3 earnings release

Our performance for the quarter met our expectations. Our sales increased, and we achieved a gross margin of 31.9%, up nearly 260 basis points from 29.3% in the same period last year, and up 140 basis points from 30.5% in the prior quarter. We achieved an increase in gross profit margin as a percentage of sales, supported by continued operational efficiencies and the favourable margin profile of our recent acquisitions

Encroachment Of Digital-Related Services Absent In The Financials

Part of our reasoning concerning our 'Hold' rating in EBF up to now was due to how the perceived demand for printed-related material would subside over time. However, by focusing on niche markets within this industry, ongoing operational excellence and by protecting its interests through the likes of stocking up on inventory due to the recent closure of the sole carbonless paper mill in the US, EBF has definitely remained current and continues to grow its margins.

Bearish investors will point to EBF's muted growth projections, but we would counter with the following. Irrespective of growth, as long as EBF can generate enough sales, which in turn convert to earnings and cash flow, then this very same cash can be used to acquire more aligned acquisitions, which restarts the earnings cycle once more. Case in Point: A company does not need to be growing aggressively to produce substantial amounts of free cash flow. Furthermore, the fact that the capital expenditure only made up 11% of EBF's operating cash flow over the past four quarters and the company's GAAP earnings are still trading inexpensively compared to historical multiples and the sector in general, we believe investors will take to the current investment case in EBF.

Dividend Discount Model

In our March commentary last year, we stamped an approx. $17 valuation on the stock in a no-growth environment. However, with 8% bottom-line growth expected in Q4, we believe it is apt to now project a new model that incorporates growth rates. We will use the following formula, where

EPDP denotes the expected dividend per share, CCE means Ennis' cost of equity, and DGR is the dividend growth rate. Firstly, with respect to dividend growth, management has been standing pat on its $0.25 per share quarterly payment for some time now. Nevertheless, considering the GAAP dividend payout ratio comes in under 60%, the decision not to increase the dividend is more by choice than anything else. In fact, if we were to multiply the GAAP retention ratio by the trailing ROE, we would get a DGR of 5.74%.

The cost of equity reflects the expected return that equity investors demand for investing their capital in EBF. Given Ennis' low beta (under-average volatility), we calculate the company's cost of equity to be approximately 5.96%.

Therefore, considering the above, where we have now incorporated EBF's ROE and retention ratio, we see that.

Value of EBF = $1.057 / 0.0596 - 0.0574 = $48.05 per share.

Risks

Obviously, being a small-cap play means trading volume is lower than its larger-cap counterparts. Aggressive moves in small caps have a greater tendency to move shares below support, all things remaining equal. However, the company's low beta of 0.37 is a testament to how investors view this play with its well-covered dividend from both a GAAP earnings and cash-flow standpoint, definitely adding to its stability.

The key question is whether EBF can continue to transform itself by successfully operating within niche markets in this industry. Up to now, the fact that ongoing acquisitions have not only led to earnings growth but also margin growth demonstrates that tuck-in purchases can essentially hit the ground running whilst saving costs in the process. Can this continue indefinitely, or will the digital revolution eventually catch up with Ennis in the long run? The areas that are immune to the digital revolution are where Ennis wants to be investing today. Areas where high concurrent levels of printing are required are where Ennis wants to control the market. So far, it is doing a good job.

Conclusion

To sum up, we are upgrading our rating in EBF to a 'Buy' on growing margins, growing cash flow, and encouraging technicals, which are pointing to a breakout above the stock's late 2024 highs. Although valuation modeling is not bulletproof, we like the distance between our projection and the prevailing share price of EBF ($20.76 per share). We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: U.S. Q4 Growth Set To Extend Streak As K-Shaped Risk Lurks
data.description:
The US economy is on track to report a third straight quarter of growth in tomorrow’s delayed GDP update for Q4, based on the median of a set of nowcasts compiled by The Capital Spectator. The pace is expected to slow from Q3, but the increase will be strong enough to keep last year’s chatter about recession on the fringes of economic analysis.

Today’s revised estimate shows output in the previous quarter rose 2.7% at an annualized rate for GDP, unchanged from the previous estimate. If this median nowcast is accurate, growth will downshift from Q3’s strong 4.4% advance, which marked a two-year high.

The widely followed GDPNow estimate from the Atlanta Fed remains the upside outlier, currently nowcasting a 3.6% increase. Using this outlook as a guide in context with our median nowcast implies that a high-2%-to-low-3% increase is a reasonable assumption for tomorrow’s release.

Although the top-line measure of economic activity is expected to chug along at a solid pace for a third straight quarter, there are growing concerns about the so-called K-economy effect - a reference to an uneven economy across sectors and households. For example, a substantial gap in consumer sentiment has opened up between consumers with equity investments compared with households with little or no stock holdings. “Sentiment surged [in February] for consumers with the largest stock portfolios, while it stagnated and remained at dismal levels for consumers without stock holdings,” survey director Joanne Hsu said.

The gap between high and low earners “leaves the economy much more sensitive,” said Samuel Tombs, chief US economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics. “It’s almost like the stock market is the tail that’s wagging the dog of the economy,” added Emily Roland, co-chief investment strategist at Manulife John Hancock Investments.

The implication: a sharp fall in the stock market could have outsized effects on the economy. That’s a risk for 2026, but the threat is expected to remain muted in tomorrow’s GDP update.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: GBP/USD Breaks Trendline; Is A 470-Odd Pip Decline On The Way? (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

GBP:USD has continued to slide, thanks in part to the US dollar resurgence this week as well as renewed hopes of rate cuts from the Bank of England (BoE).

For more on the fundamentals affecting the British pound and outlook on the economy after this week's data, read:

  • Breaking News: UK headline CPI cools, services sector inflation remains sticky. GBP/USD steady
  • Bank of England moves closer to rate cuts. March a real turning point for monetary policy and the Pound

Scenario 1

Cable has been on a downward trend, printing lower highs and lower lows since the peak of 1.38700 printed on January 27, 2026.

The pair has staircased its way lower since then, and has finally breached the medium-term ascending trendline with a daily candle close yesterday.

This sets up a potential drop of as much as 470-odd pips moving forward.

GBP/USD Daily Time Frame, February 19, 2026

Dropping down to a four-hour chart, GBP/USD is printing a fresh low, while the RSI period 14 hovers in oversold territory.

That is a concern and may hint at a pullback in the near term before a bearish continuation.

Keep an eye on the swing highs around 1.3573 and potentially 1.3651, which lines up with the 100-day MA on the H4 chart.

GBP/USD Four-Hour Time Frame, February 19, 2026

Scenario 2

The concern for this setup is the US dollar, which is enjoying a renaissance this week, which has driven a part of this breakout.

Later today, markets will focus on US data releases of initial jobless claims and the December trade surplus report.

If today’s data confirms a narrower-than-expected deficit for December, it would likely boost growth projections for the fourth quarter of 2025 and provide the US dollar with a short-term lift.

Despite this potential for a bounce, the broader outlook for the greenback remains challenged. While the US Dollar Index (DXY) may drift toward the 98.00 level on positive data, a pervasive "sell the rally" sentiment continues to dominate the market.

Many market participants expect the currency’s strength to be fleeting, as long-term expectations for Federal Reserve policy and global trade shifts maintain downward pressure on the dollar's overall trajectory.

This overarching theme around the US dollar is a major concern and could be the one to scupper a potential deeper selloff in GBP/USD.

In order for the bearish setup to be invalidated, a daily candle close above the 1.3700 swing high on the daily chart is needed to put bulls back in control.

GBP/USD Daily Time Frame, February 19, 2026

Safe trades!

Original Post

data.title: Figma: Coast Is Clear To Rebound (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In just a few short months, many software companies made the hard transition from being AI stock darlings to potential AI roadkill, at immediate risk of being unseated by cheaply vibe-coded applications. As the Q4 earnings season rolls on and we're seeing strong results and outlooks from a spectrum of software stocks, the "SaaSpocalypse" narrative continues to lose more and more credibility.

Figma (FIG), the AI-powered design platform that is taking market share from legacy incumbents like Adobe (ADBE), just soared 15% after reporting healthy Q4 results and issuing a robust outlook for FY26. Of course, those gains still do little to arrest a tremendous correction over the past year, with shares of Figma below its original $33 IPO price and well below the $100 levels at which it changed hands last August. The question for investors now is: can this stock make a full rebound?

I last wrote a neutral article on Figma in December, when the stock was trading in the high $30s. Since then, the stock is down ~30%, even after the recent post-earnings jump. Given Figma's quickly collapsing valuation amid strong growth (which provides ample evidence that AI is a tailwind rather than a headwind), I'm more than enticed to buy Figma on the dip, and I'm raising my rating on the stock to buy.

To me, especially at current decimated share prices, there are a number of bull case tailwinds that support a rebound for Figma. The key catalysts for investors to keep in mind are:

  • Figma is achieving terrific growth at scale. Figma has surpassed a $1 billion annualized revenue run rate, and yet, in spite of this, the company is still showcasing revenue growth accelerating to the high 30%'s/40%'s. This is a clear indication of a company that serves a broad TAM and has greenfield expansion opportunities, especially as it launches more products.
  • Cross-sell and retention momentum is unparalleled, especially as Figma grows its penetration among larger clients. Figma boasts net retention rates over 130%, indicating that the average Figma customer is boosting its spending on the platform by over 30% y/y. That's a powerful source of revenue growth opportunity from within the existing install base alone, not to mention net-new customer additions.
  • Rule of 40- The company balances ~40% revenue growth alongside low/mid-teens pro forma operating margins, giving Figma a "Rule of 40" score above 50, which is rarefied territory for software companies.
  • Past the lockup expiration period- We're now past the company's IPO lockup expiration period in late January, though low share prices now probably mean few insiders are willing to unload their shares. Moving past this date allows investors to focus more on fundamentals.

Originally, my biggest concern with Figma was an overstretched valuation. But now that the stock has deeply corrected since the beginning of the year, this is no longer an issue for Figma. At current post-earnings share prices near $28, Figma trades at a market cap of $13.78 billion. Netting off the $1.69 billion of cash (which includes a small, roughly ~$31 million balance of digital assets) against zero debt gives us an enterprise value of $12.09 billion.

As shown in the chart above, for FY26 Figma has guided to $1.366-$1.374 billion in revenue, reflecting a growth rate of 29-30% y/y. We note the reason that the stock rallied post-earnings is that Wall Street had expected much more modest $1.29 billion in revenue, or 22% y/y growth for the year.

Figma sits at a valuation of 8.8x EV/FY26 revenue. Of course, it's a stretch to call Figma a bargain stock in a market era where double-digit revenue multiples have become rare. But we should also consider the fact of Figma's rapid revenue growth. Even if growth slowed to, say, ~25% in FY27, Figma's valuation multiple would quickly compress to a mid-6s forward multiple of revenue - in line with many established software companies. As recently as last year, growth software stocks with over 30% top-line growth commanded mid-teens valuation multiples, illustrating how much room Figma has to rise if the market normalizes past the current fear of AI.

Stay long here and ride the post-earnings dip higher.

Q4 Download

Let's now go through Figma's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Figma's revenue grew 40% y/y to $303.8 million, smashing Wall Street's expectations of $293.1 million (+35% y/y) by a three-point margin. The wide spread of Figma reflects the growing divide between fear and reality that enterprise software stocks are set for immediate seat losses amid the growing popularity of vibe coding enterprise applications. Also, we note that Figma's revenue growth accelerated two points versus 38% y/y growth in Q3, which is quite a tough feat for a company that has reached Figma's scale.

The core driver of Figma's growth is net customer expansion. Figma continues to attract customers who end up consuming far more than they initially signed up for, which is a great illustration of how Figma's AI credits system is driving expansion. Furthermore, the company notes that customers are adopting multiple products. The company notes that an increasing proportion of Figma's users are adopting AI workflows, adding Figma Design seats on top of their existing Figma Make subscriptions. As shown in the chart below, net dollar retention improved sequentially to 136%, five points higher than Q3 at 131%. Gross revenue retention (before adding the benefits of expansion seats) clocked in at a healthy 97%.

Part of the tailwind here is that Figma continues to appeal to more and more enterprise customers, potentially signaling a shift away from legacy tools like Adobe. The company ended Q4 with 1,405 customers who generate more than $100k in ARR, a sequential add of 153 such customers (versus 133 added in Q3).

We also think Figma's growing base of enterprise customers should provide a bulwark of safety against immediate churn. Larger customers, who ingrain tools like Figma into their daily operations, are far less likely to sustain the operating risk of suddenly cutting over and moving to an internally built, potentially sloppily vibe-coded tool. Figma's accelerating revenue growth, plus its boosted traction with larger customers, serves to further highlight that AI is a net tailwind rather than a risk for the company.

Where we do find Figma a little wanting is in its profit expansion. Pro forma operating margins lost 10 points y/y to 14%, as the company bears the burden of public company costs plus expands its headcount to fuel its growth opportunity.

We're also disappointed that the company's FY26 operating income target of just $100-$110 million implies just an 8% operating margin next year, a slide from 12% full-year margins in FY25. We think the company is setting guidance conservatively and will eventually exceed these targets, but we do think it's critical for Figma to maintain its "Rule of 40" orientation in order to justify an upward correction in its valuation multiples.

Key Takeaways

There's a lot to like about Figma as it heads into FY26 with a robust growth outlook and a rapidly expanding base of customers. Investors have a tremendous chance to buy this rarefied growth stock at a single-digit revenue multiple. Though we're hoping for greater indications of Figma scaling profitably as it grows, I think this is a great juncture to buy Figma on the dip.

data.title: A More Durable Robinhood Emerges (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Robinhood’s (HOOD) business is moving away from the trading-focused business it was originally founded on and becoming more of an asset-gathering business, subscription business, and banking business. This makes the business more stable and less dependent on the ups and downs of cryptocurrency or trading markets.

Increasing deposits will continue to grow the business’s interest income and operating leverage, providing a stable earnings profile. If the company can continue to drive the strong deposit growth into 2026, the business mix will continue to improve. Since my last coverage, when I downgraded the stock to Hold, shares have fallen 26%, but with the business now more diversified and resilient, I believe the risk/reward supports an upgrade to Buy.

Targets 20%+ Net Deposit Growth in FY26

As I see it, Robinhood Markets has expanding net deposits and a diversified topline. This base reduces dependence on trading volumes that are volatile in nature. Robinhood Markets has moved beyond simple transaction-based income into a broad ecosystem.

In FY25, total platform assets grew nearly ~70% YoY to $324 billion. Net deposits hit $68 billion, which represents a 35% growth rate. This influx of capital directly supports financials by increasing the base of interest-earning assets. Interest-earning assets increased 39% amid growth in the cash sweep program, margin, and credit card loan books.

The expansion into subscription models offers stability. The subscribers of Robinhood Gold grew 58% YoY to 4.2 million, while subscription revenue grew 56% to $50 million in Q4. This is a hedge against market downturns. I mean, as market volatility decreases and trading volumes decrease, subscription fees remain unchanged.

Robinhood Markets now operates 11 businesses, deriving over $100 million in annualized topline. In my view, this diversification means that a decline in one sector (like cryptocurrency) may not destabilize the entire topline. Crypto revenue dropped 38% YoY to $221 million. Against this drop, total topline grew 27% to $1.3 billion, and it confirms that Robinhood Markets can stabilize growth even when specific asset classes underperform.

Over 80% of revenue now comes from outside of crypto. The shift minimizes the risk linked with digital asset cycles. Product expansion drives wallet share. The launch of retirement accounts, joint accounts, and banking services supports customers in consolidating assets on HOOD’s platform.

Retirement assets under custody increased 102% to $26.5 billion. Banking adoption indicates solid initial results. Over 50% of funded banking customers enroll in direct deposit. This high attachment rate signals that customers use Robinhood Markets as a primary financial hub instead of only for speculative trading.

Moreover, operational leverage improves as assets are scaling. The TTM gross profit margin is at 92.4%. It is higher than the sector median of 60.31%. This margin profile indicates that Robinhood Markets can add to the top line with minimal incremental cost. As net deposits grow, the interest income derived from these assets flows to the bottom line. The cash sweep program jumped 26% to $32.8 billion. Whereas the margin book increased 113% to $16.8 billion.

These balances derive high-margin interest income. International expansion adds another element of growth. Robinhood Markets launched services in the UK and EU and acquired 0.75 million international customers. Robinhood plans to expand into additional jurisdictions, boosting the total addressable market, and the focus on new markets in 2026 and active traders also supports volume.

Further, equity notional trading volumes increased 68% (to $710 billion). Options contracts traded boosted 38% to 659 million. By targeting active traders with products like futures and index options, Robinhood Markets captures higher trading activity per user. Forward financials benefit from this asset accumulation as interest rates fluctuate; a larger asset base is a buffer.

If trading volumes decline, the interest on $32.8 billion in cash sweeps continues to derive topline. In my opinion, if crypto markets freeze, the 4.2 million Gold subscribers will continue paying fees. The move from a transaction-only model to an asset-gathering model creates more predictable earnings.

As I look forward, the 2026 plan targets another year of 20%+ net deposit growth. Hitting this target increases the base of assets available for monetization. Robinhood targets building a global ecosystem through integrating banking, retirement, and trading. With that, Robinhood Markets increases customer retention. Sticky assets that stay on the platform longer may continue to generate lifetime value.

Revenue Must Exceed 18% Expense Growth to Stabilize 30x P/E

For me, Robinhood Markets has a risk where its market valuation is over its near-term financial delivery capabilities. This is boosted by continued volatility in transactional revenue. A forward P/E (non-GAAP) of 29.4x. This is 175% higher than the sector median of 10.6x. This means that the market demands high growth when financials miss these elevated expectations. HOOD's stock price suffers a big correction, and the Q4 points to this risk.

Robinhood Markets Q4 net revenue of $1.28 billion had missed the consensus estimate of $1.34 billion by ~$60 million. Following this, HOOD stock declined ~8%-10%. The miss came from a decline in transaction-based revenue that lagged analyst estimates. Transaction-based revenue remains a central base of the financial model (totaling $776 million).

Moreover, as I see it, cryptocurrency revenue proves this volatility. Crypto revenue fell 38% YoY to $221 million despite Robinhood Markets maintaining a bullish long-term stance on the industry. When Bitcoin prices/general crypto interest wanes, this topline contracts quickly. Robinhood already acknowledged that falling Bitcoin prices impacted Q4 earnings. So, the dependence on market interest for specific asset classes leads to earnings unpredictability. Expense growth presents another issue to financials.

Robinhood issued guidance for 2026 adjusted OpEx and share-based compensation to range between $2.6 billion and $2.725 billion. This range is an 18% increase at the midpoint against 2025. Rising costs require supporting topline growth to maintain margins. If transaction volumes soften and asset prices decline, topline growth may fail to catch this 18% expense hike.

Further, operating leverage works in both ways. A high-gross-margin structure benefits from volume increases but is hurt as volume drops. Fixed costs for engineering, compliance, and infra remain high regardless of trading activity. If the topline stagnates due to a crypto winter/reduced retail participation, margins compress. Thus, GAAP EPS may have missed estimates by failing to match the revenue projections. Although EPS did beat by $0.02 on a GAAP basis due to other factors.

However, the revenue miss drives the issue. CapEx efficiency also lags the sector median. The CapEx to Sales ratio is 0.34%, which is 79.88% lower than the sector median. Although this suggests a capital-light model, the D- grade implies that investment in physical infra/necessary upgrades might not be enough to match industry standards. Alternatively, it may also indicate that growth relies heavily on marketing and personnel (OPEX) instead of capitalized assets, which keeps ongoing costs high.

Finally, I think the Valuation Grade of D overall points out that HOOD stock is expensive across multiple metrics. Forward P/S is 12.54x, which is 330% higher than the sector median. Overall, these multiples indicate a little room for error. For instance, any deceleration in net deposit growth/active trader participation can trigger a re-rating of the stock. Again, if the market money rotates out of high-growth, high-multiple stocks, Robinhood Markets stock may be hit with downside even amid its operational progress.

Takeaway

Robinhood is transforming into an asset-gathering platform, with 35% deposit growth, 80%+ revenue from outside crypto, and 92% gross margins providing stronger operating leverage. The key is sustaining 20%+ deposit growth into 2026. With a 26% pullback, the improved mix should provide more long-term stability, although high valuation leaves little room for execution error.
